coredata.dc:title: Amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots based ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor for ultrasensitive and highly selective recognition of horseradish peroxidase
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Shan
Wang, Lumin
Huang, Chusheng
Su, Wei
Xiao, Qi
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor for ultrasensitive and highly selective recognition of horseradish peroxidase (HRP) was reported for the first time, by employing amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots (af-GQDs) as the reference fluorophore and 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP) as the specific response signal. DAP was the oxidation product of o-phenylenediamine (OPD) that acted as the cosubstrate of HRP. Upon the addition of HRP, OPD could be catalytically oxidized to DAP, then the fluorescence intensity corresponding to DAP at 553nm increased dramatically with a simultaneous fluorescence quenching of af-GQDs at 440nm, resulting in a ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor toward HRP. This ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor exhibited a broad linear range and excellent sensitivity toward HRP detection. The fluorescence intensity ratio of DAP to af-GQDs linearly increased with the increasing of HRP concentration in the range of 2 fM–800 fM (0.02μUmL−1–8μUmL−1) with a detection limit down to 0.21 fM (2.1nUmL−1). The present ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor also showed excellent selectivity for HRP over some amino acids, nucleotides, and other heme-containing proteins. The enzymatic activity of HRP was further evaluated by quantitatively calculating the enzymatic velocity and Michaelis−Menten kinetic parameter.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots
Ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor
Horseradish peroxidase
Recognition
Enzymatic activity

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Collaborative Innovation Center of Southwest Ethnic Medicine
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Collaborative Innovation Center for Cardiovascular Disease Translational Medicine

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Institut National de la Recherche Scientifique
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Institut national de la recherche scientifique

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Guangxi Colleges and Universities Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Natural Functional Molecular Chemistry
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Natural Functional Molecular Chemistry
Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21403039 , 21563006 ), Guangxi Natural Science Foundation ( 2013GXNSFCA019005 , 2015GXNSFAA139033 ), Collaborative Innovation Center of Southwest Ethnic Medicine (Guangxi Normal University, CICSEM 2013-B6), and Guangxi Colleges and Universities Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Natural Functional Molecular Chemistry, Guangxi Teachers Education University. Appendix A

The following are Supplementary data to this article: Shan Huang received her PhD degree in Analytical Chemistry from Wuhan University in 2009. After a two-year postdoctoral (2009–2011) stay at the Institut Armand-Frappier, Institut National de la Recherche Scientifique (INRS) and INRS-Énergie, Matériaux et Télécommunications, Université du Québec, Canada, she returned to China as an associate professor at Guangxi Teachers Education University. Her current research interests are the synthesis of functional nanomaterials and application in biochemical sensors. Lumin Wang is pursuing her master degree in Analytical Chemistry at Guangxi Teachers Education University. She works on the development of novel nanomaterials for fluorescent sensors. Chusheng Huang received his PhD degree in Organic Chemistry from Lanzhou University in 1998. Now, he is a professor at Guangxi Teachers Education University. His current research interests are the synthesis of natural products and their application in chemical sensors. Wei Su received his PhD degree in Inorganic Chemistry from Shandong University in 2007. Now, he is a professor at Guangxi Teachers Education University. His current research interests are the synthesis of functional ruthenium anticancer drugs and their interactions with DNA. Qi Xiao received his PhD degree in Physical Chemistry from Wuhan University in 2010. After a one-year postdoctoral (2010–2011) stay at University of Massachusetts, USA, he returned to China as a professor at Guangxi Teachers Education University. His current research interests are the synthesis of functional graphene nanomaterials and their application in biological sensors.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–vis absorption spectra of af-GQDs, excitation spectrum of af-GQDs at λ em =440nm and emission spectrum of af-GQDs at λ ex =346nm. Insert: Photograph of af-GQDs under the radiation of white light (Left) and 365nm UV lamp (Right). (B) Fluorescence spectra of af-GQDs under different excitation wavelength. (C) Fluorescence decay trace of af-GQDs. The measurement was made at λ em =440nm. τ is the fluorescence lifetime of af-GQDs, and b is the normalized preexponential factor, respectively. (D) FT-IR spectrum of af-GQDs. (E) TEM image of af-GQDs. (F) Diameter size distribution curve of af-GQDs.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of af-GQDs, DAP, af-GQDs-DAP complex, and difference absorption spectrum between af-GQDs-DAP complex and af-GQDs. af-GQDs: 13.2μgmL−1; DAP: 60nM. (B) Fluorescence decay trace of af-GQDs before (a) and after addition of DAP (b). af-GQDs: 13.2μgmL−1; DAP: 10μM. (C) Fluorescence spectra of af-GQDs under different substances and DAP alone. af-GQDs: 13.2μgmL−1; HRP: 500 fM; H2O2: 5mM; OPD: 3mM; DAP: 3mM. (D) Fluorescence spectra of af-GQDs, af-GQDs-OPD-H2O2 system, and af-GQDs-OPD-H2O2 system with different concentrations of HRP. af-GQDs: 13.2μgmL−1; OPD: 3mM; H2O2: 5mM.
Influences of the amount of af-GQDs (A), concentrations of OPD (B), concentrations of H2O2 (C), pH value of PBS (D), incubation temperature (E), and reaction time (F).

(A) Fluorescence spectra of af-GQDs-OPD-H2O2 system in different concentrations of HRP. a–j: 0, 2, 5, 10, 30, 60, 200, 400, 600, 800 fM (0, 0.02, 0.05, 0.1, 0.3, 0.6, 2, 4, 6, 8μUmL−1). af-GQDs: 13.2μgmL−1; OPD: 3mM; H2O2: 5mM; 10mM PBS: pH 6.5. (B) Photograph of the ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor in the presence of different HRP concentrations under the radiation of white light (Upper) and 365nm UV lamp (Below). a–j: 0, 2, 5, 10, 30, 60, 200, 400, 600, 800 fM (0, 0.02, 0.05, 0.1, 0.3, 0.6, 2, 4, 6, 8μUmL−1). (C) Linear relationship between fluorescence intensity ratio I 553/I 440 and HRP concentration in the range of 2 fM–800 fM (0.02μUmL−1–8μUmL−1). (D) Enzymatic activity derived from assaying a constant amount of DAP with an increasing concentration of HRP. Error bars represent standard deviations from triplicate measurements.
Schematic illustration of HRP determination using af-GQDs-based ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor.
Comparison of methods for the determination of HRP.
Effect of coexisting foreign substances for HRP determination. a

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: af-GQDs: 13.2μgmL−1; OPD: 3mM; H2O2: 5mM; HRP: 800 fM (8μUmL−1); PBS: 10mM, pH 6.5.

head.ce:title: Amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots based ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor for ultrasensitive and highly selective recognition of horseradish peroxidase

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Guangxi Teachers Education University, Nanning 530001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Collaborative Innovation Center of Southwest Ethnic Medicine, Guangxi Normal University, Guilin, 541004, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Collaborative Innovation Center of Southwest Ethnic Medicine

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Guangxi Teachers Education University, Nanning 530001, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor for ultrasensitive and highly selective recognition of horseradish peroxidase (HRP) was reported for the first time, by employing amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots (af-GQDs) as the reference fluorophore and 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP) as the specific response signal. DAP was the oxidation product of o-phenylenediamine (OPD) that acted as the cosubstrate of HRP. Upon the addition of HRP, OPD could be catalytically oxidized to DAP, then the fluorescence intensity corresponding to DAP at 553nm increased dramatically with a simultaneous fluorescence quenching of af-GQDs at 440nm, resulting in a ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor toward HRP. This ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor exhibited a broad linear range and excellent sensitivity toward HRP detection. The fluorescence intensity ratio of DAP to af-GQDs linearly increased with the increasing of HRP concentration in the range of 2 fM–800 fM (0.02μUmL−1–8μUmL−1) with a detection limit down to 0.21 fM (2.1nUmL−1). The present ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor also showed excellent selectivity for HRP over some amino acids, nucleotides, and other heme-containing proteins. The enzymatic activity of HRP was further evaluated by quantitatively calculating the enzymatic velocity and Michaelis−Menten kinetic parameter.

ce:section.ce:para: As an upstart of carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have attracted tremendous attentions since their discovery [1–3]. Compared with some semiconductor nanocrystals including toxic/expensive heavy-metals, GQDs are superior in terms of excellent photostability, low cytotoxicity, exceptional biocompatibility, and high water solubility. Therefore, GQDs are expected as the most promising candidates for designing novel fluorescent probes for some chemical and biological assays [4–6]. Recently, ascribed to the significantly boosted quantum yield compared with normal GQDs, amino-functionalized GQDs (af-GQDs) have attracted increasing attention in diverse research fields. Tetsuka and co-workers reported the optically tunable af-GQDs with clear multiple colors and higher luminance quantum yields [7,8]. Chattopadhyay et al. revealed the origin of tunable heterogeneous photoluminescence of af-GQDs from theoretical and experimental analysis [9]. Qu’s group fabricated highly photoluminescent af-GQDs for sensing Cu2+ ions in aqueous solutions and living cells [10]. Since Cu2+ ion could bind and chelate with N and O on the surface of af-GQDs than other transition metal ions, the selectivity of af-GQDs for Cu2+ ion was much higher than that of normal GQDs. These results indicate that af-GQDs can be selective for many analytes due to their higher quantum yield and abundant functionalized surface groups.

Based on the reversible fluorescence variation of GQDs during detection process, GQDs have been widely utilized as fluorescent nanosensors for some specific targets through fluorescence “on-off-on” mode [11–13]. However, such single-wavelength-based detecting signal can be easily interfered with various factors, for example, optical path length, drift of light source or detector, and environmental conditions of samples, which otherwise limit the accuracy and sensitivity of quantitative determination. Fortunately, ratiometric fluorescent strategy, which allows the measurement of the changes in the ratio of fluorescence intensities at two well-resolved wavelengths, possesses advantages in terms of improved sensitivity and accuracy [14,15]. Till now, dual-emission carbon-based nanohybrid materials are designed and utilized as ratiometric fluorescent probes for different substances. Qian’s group have developed GQDs-based core-satellite ratiometric fluorescence probe for visual determination of Hg2+ ions [16]. Song et al. developed a ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor with carbon dots@europium-based nanoscale coordination polymers for biological warfare agent detection [17]. Shan and coworkers also prepared carbon quantum dots/gold nanoclusters nanohybrid-based ratiometric fluorescent probe for sensitive and selective sensing of Ca2+ ions and l-ascorbic acid [18]. Our group also reported a ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor based on fluorescent carbon dots for highly selective recognition of DNA [19]. As a matter of fact, these ratiometric fluorescence measurements can not only efficiently avoid the potential interferences from background fluorescence but also drastically improve the sensitivity of the detection, which undoubtedly exploit the practical application of these carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials in biochemical and biomedical detections to certain extent.

Horseradish peroxidase (HRP), which possesses highly efficient catalysis ability on the decomposition of peroxide, is one of most widely used enzyme reagent in analytical applications [20,21]. Several analytical strategies have been established for HRP determination, such as UV–vis spectrometry [22], colorimetric assay [23], square wave voltammetry [24], as well as chemiluminescence methods [25,26]. Among these techniques, o-phenylenediamine (OPD), hydroquinone, pyrocatechol, p-chlorophenol, and tetramethylbenzidine were evaluated as cosubstrates of HRP and could react with HRP to produce different substances with absorption or electrochemical signals. Particularly, OPD was selected as the best cosubstrate for HRP, since the HRP/hydrogen peroxide (HRP/H2O2)-catalyzed oxidation of colorless OPD could lead to the formation of various colored products, especially fluorescent 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP) whose maximum excitation/emission wavelengths of 430nm/553nm [22,24].

Recent literatures have shown that carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials have been successfully utilized to establish fluorescent assays for several enzymes activity evaluation, such as phosphatase and acetylcholinesterase [27–29]. Inspired by these facts, we fabricated an ultrasensitive and highly selective ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor for HRP determination by using af-GQDs (emission wavelength of 440nm) as the reference fluorophore and DAP as the specific response signal. It has been reported that the π-network of thick planar sheets in sp2-bonded GQDs can provide a good substrate for anchoring π-conjugated molecules through π–π stacking interaction [30], which is very efficient for fluorescent quenching of af-GQDs by DAP in our system. As shown in Scheme 1 , when OPD is oxidized by HRP to form DAP, the fluorescence intensity corresponding to DAP at 553nm increases significantly with a simultaneous fluorescence quenching of af-GQDs at 440nm, resulting in a ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor toward HRP. Such hybridized ratiometric nanosensor has been demonstrated ultrasensitive and highly selective for practical bioapplication such as on-site and visual detection of HRP.

1.0mgmL−1 af-GQDs were purchased from Nanjing XFNANO Materials Tech. Co., Ltd. (http://www.xfnano.com/). HRP (>250Umg−1), OPD, DAP, amino acids, nucleotides, and other proteins were obtained from Sigma (St. Louis, MO, USA). Hydrogen peroxide (30%, w/w) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All other reagents were of analytical-reagent grade and used as received. 10mM PBS was prepared by mixing stock standard solutions of NaH2PO4 and Na2HPO4. Ultrapure water with resistivity of 18.2MΩcm was produced by passing through RiOs 8 unit followed by Millipore-Q Academic purification set (Millipore, Bedford, MA, USA).

UV-vis absorption spectra were recorded on Cary 100 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, Inc., Australia). Fluorescence spectra were measured with RF-5301 PC luminescence spectrometer (Shimadzu Co., Ltd., Tokyo, Japan). Time-resolved fluorescence decay traces were recorded with Fluorolog-3 system (Horiba Jobin Yvon, France) by using an excitation wavelength of 374nm. FT-IR spectra were recorded on Nicolet iS10 spectrometer (Thermo, USA). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were taken by JEM 2011FEF high-resolution transmission electron microscope (JEOL Ltd., Tokyo, Japan). All pH measurements were made with a basic pH meter PB-10 (Sartorius Scientific Instruments Co., Ltd., Beijing, China).

In a typical run for the determination of HRP, 1.5mL of 10mM PBS (pH 6.5), 0.3mL of 30mM OPD solution, 0.5mL of 30mM H2O2 solution, and appropriate aliquot of HRP solution were transferred into a 5mL eppendorf tube and then the mixture was thoroughly stirred at 25°C for 20min in dark. After that, 40μL of 1.0mgmL−1 af-GQDs solution was added into the mixture and finally diluted to 3mL with 10mM PBS (pH 6.5). After 1min incubation at 25°C in dark, the fluorescence spectra were measured for quantitative analysis of HRP.

For fluorescence detection, the band-slits of excitation and emission were set as 15.0nm and 5.0nm, respectively. The sensitivity was fixed on high and the response time was set as 0.5s. The fluorescence spectra were recorded from 360nm to 680nm under excitation wavelength of 346nm. The fluorescence intensities of af-GQDs at 440nm (I 440) and DAP at 553nm (I 553) were recorded. The ratiometric fluorescence signal variations at I 553/I 440 were utilized for label-free, ultrasensitive and highly selective determination of HRP.

As shown in Fig. 1 A, af-GQDs exhibited broad absorption band with a typical absorption peak centered at around 330nm, which was ascribed to the presence of amino edge functional groups and π–π ∗ electronic transition in CC sp2 carbon domains of carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials that resulted in the strong fluorescence of af-GQDs [7,9]. Furthermore, the fluorescence spectra indicated that af-GQDs exhibited an obvious, narrow and symmetrical fluorescence spectrum with a strong emission peak centered at 440nm under 346nm excitation, with a Stokes shift of 94nm. As shown in the digital pictures inserted in Fig. 1A, the diluted af-GQDs solution was almost colorless observed by the naked eyes under ambient daylight but emitted very strong blue fluorescence under the radiation of 365nm UV lamp, demonstrating the higher photoluminescent property of these af-GQDs. The relative quantum yield (QY) of af-GQDs was examined by using quinine sulfate (QY=54% in 0.1M H2SO4 solution) as the standard substance. The results showed that the relative QY of af-GQDs was calculated to be about 10% under 346nm excitation (Fig. S1).

As observed from most of carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials, af-GQDs undoubtedly exhibited the identical excitation-dependent fluorescence property, due to the different surface state emissive trap of these carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials [7,9]. As exhibited in Fig. 1B, with the excitation wavelength increased from 310nm to 390nm, the maximum fluorescence peak position of af-GQDs shifted to the long wavelength direction obviously, and the fluorescence peak intensity of af-GQDs increased firstly and decreased subsequently. These results elucidated that the maximum excitation/emission wavelengths of af-GQDs were at 346nm/440nm, respectively.

The fluorescence lifetime of af-GQDs was investigated by time-resolved fluorescence spectrometry. The fluorescence data were fitted well with biexponential equation and the average fluorescence lifetime was given by the sum of ∑b i τ i products [31]. As exhibited in Fig. 1C, the average fluorescence lifetime of af-GQDs was calculated to be about 6.29ns. In addition, the stability of af-GQDs was investigated by long-time monitoring of fluorescence intensity at 440nm, and the results suggested that af-GQDs exhibited remarkable photostability for nearly 2h at room temperature (Fig. S2). The influence of pH on the fluorescence intensity of af-GQDs was also researched. The fluorescence intensity of af-GQDs at 440nm varied obviously when the pH values increased from 3 to 10 (Fig. S3), which suggested that the fluorescence property of af-GQDs strongly depended on pH value. In addition, the fluorescence intensity of af-GQDs did not change obviously even in 15M NaCl solution (Fig. S4). These entire phenomena suggested that af-GQDs were excellent candidates as powerful and appropriate probes in biological applications.

The functional groups present on the surface of af-GQDs can be identified by FT-IR spectrometry. As indicated in Fig. 1D, the absorption peaks at 3431cm−1 and 3130cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibration of NH bonds [7]. The absorption peaks at 1400cm−1 and 1576cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibrations of CN bonds and the bending vibrations of N−H bonds, respectively [7]. The absorption peak at 1664cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of HNCO bonds [10]. All these absorption bands indicated the existence of amines and amides. Therefore, it was supposed that there was no significant basal plane functionalization, and hence the amino groups might be preponderantly located in the edges of graphene layers. Electron-donating amino groups have a better ability to donate electrons than hydroxyl groups and can further enhance the luminescence of af-GQDs.

The typical TEM image showed that these af-GQDs were well-dispersed (Fig. 1E). The size distributions of af-GQDs were in the range of 2nm–4.5nm, and the average sizes of af-GQDs were around 3.2nm (Fig. 1F). The image indicated that these af-GQDs were crystalline, implying that these af-GQDs maintained similar crystallinity to graphene [10].

As illustrated in Scheme 1, the primary π-network at the surface of af-GQDs plays the role of a recognition receptor of DAP by the strong specific π–π stacking interaction and electrostatic adhesion. Thus, DAP is attached to the surface of af-GQDs to form af-GQDs-DAP complex, which will lead to the fluorescence quenching of af-GQDs and the fluorescence enhancement of DAP. This mechanism can be expounded by UV–vis spectrometry. Fig. 2 A showed that the UV–vis absorption peak of af-GQDs had an obvious blue shift (from 334nm to 328nm) when 60nM DAP was present. On the other hand, the UV–vis absorption peak of DAP exhibited an obvious red shift (from 426nm to 432nm) when af-GQDs was present. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of DAP and the difference absorption spectra between af-GQDs-DAP complex and af-GQDs were different within experimental error, indicating the ground state complex formation of af-GQDs with DAP and the static quenching mechanism of af-GQDs-DAP system. The local environmental variations of the af-GQDs-DAP complex lead to the absorbance behavior of the system, which indicates common fluorescence quenching with DAP at the surface of af-GQDs. In addition, the average fluorescence lifetimes of af-GQDs before and after addition of 10μM DAP were 6.29ns and 7.08ns, respectively (Fig. 2B). Therefore, we can interpret the fluorescence behavior of the system as a result of the changed local environment by the complexation reaction between af-GQDs and DAP.

Anti-interference ability is another important parameter to evaluate the performance of this ratiometric fluorescent nanosensing system. It is widely-reported that OPD can react with HRP/H2O2 to produce colored DAP [22,24], the interferences of these co-existing substances on HRP detection must be researched thoroughly. As expected, the fluorescence intensity at 440nm contributed by af-GQDs changed slightly in the presence of OPD, H2O2, and HRP, respectively (Fig. 2C). In addition, preliminary results indicated that the fluorescence intensity of af-GQDs at 440nm was drastically quenched at the present of DAP, and the fluorescence intensity of DAP at 553nm was significantly increased when af-GQDs was present, which led to a ratiometric fluorescent change between af-GQDs and DAP (Fig. S5). The response to fluorescence quenching of various concentration of DAP was analyzed using the Stern-Volmere equation [32]: I 0/I =1+ K q τ 0[Q]=1+ K SV[Q]. Herein, I 0 and I are the fluorescence intensities of af-GQDs at 440nm in the absence and presence of DAP, respectively, K q represents the bimolecular quenching constant, τ 0 is the lifetime of af-GQDs in the absence of DAP, K SV is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, and [Q] is the concentration of DAP. Fig. S6 shows the Stern-Volmer plots of I 0/I versus [DAP]. The resulted plot exhibited a good linear relationship in the concentration range of 50nM–1.2μM (R 2 =0.9987). Stern-Volmer quenching constant K SV, which was calculated by linear regression of the plot, was 3.02×106 M−1. For af-GQDs-DAP system, τ 0 is known to be 6.29ns, thus K q is calculated to be 4.8×1014 M−1 s−1. It has been reported that the maximum value of K q for diffusion controlled quenching process is about 1.0×1010 M−1 s−1 [32], so the higher value of K q obtained in this system suggested that the fluorescence quenching of af-GQDs occurred by a specific interaction between af-GQDs and DAP. This also implied that the dominating fluorescence quenching mechanism was the formation of af-GQDs-DAP complex, which further confirmed the highest fluorescence sensitivity and chemical selectivity for DAP.

Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 2D, the fluorescence intensity of af-GQDs at 440nm could not change dramatically even when OPD and H2O2 were all present in solution. However, the fluorescence intensity of af-GQDs at 440nm in af-GQDs-OPD-H2O2 system was quenched gradually with the increase of the concentration of HRP. 10 fM (0.1μUmL−1) and 60 fM (0.6μUmL−1) of HRP presented a remarkable quenching effect of 35% and 71% on af-GQDs, respectively. Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity of DAP at 553nm increased by about six or eleven times. Therefore, the fluorescence intensity ratio I 553/I 440 was varied tremendously with the increasing of HRP concentration. It was resulted that the fluorescence intensity ratio I 553/I 440 was mainly HRP concentration dependent and such ratiometric fluorescence approach could be used for the ultrasensitive detection of HRP.

Before quantitative analysis of HRP using the proposed strategy, we optimized the detection conditions, including the amount of af-GQDs, the concentrations of OPD and H2O2, pH value of PBS, incubation temperature, and reaction time. In this study, the fluorescence intensity ratio I 553/I 440 was used as standard and 2 pM (20μUmL−1) of HRP was chosen for condition optimization. All control experiments were carried out under the same conditions.

In order to obtain high sensitivity of this ratiometic fluorescent nanosensor, the influence of af-GQDs amount was firstly considered. According to Fig. 3 A, the fluorescence intensity ratio I 553/I 440 increased obviously when the amount of af-GQDs was in the range of 3.3μgmL−1–13.2μgmL−1. However, as the more af-GQDs were added, the ratiometric fluorescent signal decreased prominently. Higher amount of af-GQDs would disturb the stability of af-GQDs and cause the aggregation of af-GQDs themselves, which might affect the ratiometric fluorescent signal and lower the sensitivity of this ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor. Since the maximum changes of the fluorescence intensity ratio I 553/I 440 appeared at 13.2μgmL−1 af-GQDs, 13.2μgmL−1 af-GQDs was applied to the detection of HRP in this study.

Secondly, the concentrations of OPD and H2O2 could affect the total amount of the produced DAP that directly related with the sensitivity of HRP detection. Therefore, the concentrations of OPD and H2O2 were evaluated undoubtedly. As shown in Fig. 3B, the fluorescence intensity ratio I 553/I 440 increased substantially with the increasing concentration of OPD, and then the ratiometric fluorescent signal almost reached the steady value when the concentration of OPD was more than 3mM. Moreover, the concentration of H2O2 exhibited same variation trend as that of OPD. As illustrated in Fig. 3C, the ratiometric fluorescent signal remained almost constant when the concentration of H2O2 was more than 2.5mM. Nevertheless, the molar ratio of OPD to H2O2 was 2:3 according to the reaction equation shown in Scheme 1 [22]. For this reason, and taking into account the decomposition of H2O2, 3mM OPD and 5mM H2O2 were selected as the reaction reagents in this work.

The sensitivity of HRP detection was mainly dependent on the catalytic activity of HRP, and the catalytic activity of HRP could be affected largely by the pH value of PBS. Herein, the influence of pH value on HRP determination was evaluated at different pH values of PBS ranging from 4 to 10. As results shown in Fig. 3D, the fluorescence intensity ratio I 553/I 440 increased gradually with the increasing pH value, which probably due to the pH-dependence catalytic property of HRP [21]. When the pH value of PBS was higher than 6.5, the ratiometric fluorescent signal decreased sharply. It was found that the maximum value of the ratiometric fluorescent signal was observed when pH value was 6.5. In strong acidic medium, the amino groups on the surface of af-GQDs could be protonated, which could decrease the fluorescence intensity of af-GQDs (Fig. S3). When the pH value of PBS was higher than 7, H2O2 was more prone to decomposing, which led to the decreasing fluorescence intensity ratio I 553/I 440. Moreover, the optimal pH value reported for HRP with OPD as the substrate was around pH 5 [33]. Considering the fact, 10mM PBS with pH 6.5 was chosen for subsequent detection.

Incubation temperature could also affect the catalytic activity of HRP, so the influence of incubation temperature was investigated at different temperature ranging from 5°C to 50°C. As indicated in Fig. 3E, when temperature increased from 5°C to 25°C, the fluorescence intensity ratio I 553/I 440 increased gradually. When the incubation temperature was higher than 25°C, the ratiometric fluorescent signal decreased accordingly. Higher temperature could accelerate the decomposing of H2O2 and reduce the catalytic activity of HRP, which resulted in the lower value of fluorescence intensity ratio I 553/I 440. Therefore, an incubation temperature of 25°C was chosen as the optimal temperature for the following detections.

Finally, the kinetic behaviors of this af-GQDs-based nanosensor system in the presence of HRP were investigated by monitoring the fluorescence intensity ratio I 553/I 440 as a function of reaction time. As indicated in Fig. 3F, the fluorescence intensity ratio I 553/I 440 increased gradually and then remained almost constant during 20min reaction time. In other words, the catalytic reaction of HRP with H2O2 for OPD approached equilibrium within 20min, thus the reaction time of 20min was used in the further experiments.

Furthermore, the effect of ionic intensity on HRP detection was also studied by choosing NaCl as the ionic strength adjustor (Fig. S7). Higher concentration of Na+ or Cl− could disturb the determination of HRP to some extent, so NaCl was not added in this detection system and the HRP determination was under a lower ionic strength condition.

To evaluate the ability of this ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor to sensitively quantify biomolecules, a series of different concentrations of HRP ranging from 2 fM (0.02μUmL−1) to 800 fM (8μUmL−1) were measured under the above optimized conditions. Fig. 4 A displayed the typical fluorescence spectra of af-GQDs-OPD-H2O2 system as functions of increasing concentration of HRP. As displayed in this figure, it was obvious that the fluorescence intensity of af-GQDs at 440nm was quenched continuously with the gradual increase of the fluorescence intensity of DAP at 553nm upon the addition of HRP with increasing concentrations, indicating that obvious ratiometic fluorescence variation was mainly dependent on the concentration of HRP. In addition, based on the internal standard of the green emission of DAP, a slight decrease of the blue emission of af-GQDs led to an obviously distinguishable color evolution from blue to yellow in the HRP concentration range of 2 fM–800 fМ (0.02μUmL−1–8μUmL−1) (Fig. 4B). A simple and low-cost visual assay for ultrasensitive detection of HRP can be thus recognized with the naked eyes without any sophisticated instrumentations and specially equipped laboratories.

The plot of the fluorescence intensity ratio I 553/I 440 against the concentration of added HRP was also shown in Fig. 4C. The fluorescence intensity ratio I 553/I 440 exhibited a good linear response to the HRP concentration in the range of 2 fM–800 fM (0.02μUmL−1–8μUmL−1), which was described by the regression equation of I 553/I 440 =0.087+1.6×1013 [HRP] with a correlation coefficient of 0.9997. The relative standard deviation for five replicate measurements of 30 fM (0.3μUmL−1) HRP was 1.6%. Based on three times standard deviation of 11 measurements of ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor without HRP, the limit of detection for HRP was calculated to be 0.21 fM (2.1nUmL−1), which was much lower than other previously reported values. As can be seen from Table 1 , the present ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor for HRP sensing possessed a broader linear range and a lower detection limit, which was comparable to the most sensitive approach reported for HRP determination.

Furthermore, the enzymatic activity of HRP was evaluated according to the reported method [24,34]. Fig. 4D showed the plot of the molar concentration of DAP generated per unit of reaction time (velocity) versus an increasing concentration of HRP. Changes in the fluorescence signal were converted into velocity using standard (or calibration) curves correlating to the concentration of DAP per af-GQDs. Using standard Michaelis–Menten kinetic analysis, parameters including the Michaelis constant K M, maximum proteolytic velocity V max, turnover number K cat, and catalytic efficiency K cat/K M were determined. It is well-known that high V max and low K M indicate high affinity of the substrates for HRP enzyme. As listed in Fig. 4D, the V max value was 3.1μMmin−1 and the K M value was 0.18μM (1.8UmL−1), suggesting that HRP demonstrated best kinetic characteristic during this catalytic reaction [35]. Furthermore, the turnover number K cat value was 0.66min−1 and the catalytic efficiency K cat/K M was 3.6×106 M−1 min−1 (0.36mLU−1 min−1), respectively. All these results proved that HRP remained high enzymatic activity in this ratiometric fluorescent nanosensing assay.

Besides sensitivity, selectivity is another important parameter to evaluate the performance of this ratiometric fluorescent nanosensing system. For the sake of the applicability of this ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor for HRP determination in biological samples, the influences of some biomolecules were investigated. If common biomolecules cause relative errors of less than±5% in the fluorescence intensity ratio I 553/I 440 of the system, these biomolecules have no obvious interference in the determination of HRP. With the addition of different biomolecules, the fluorescence intensity ratio I 553/I 440 of the nanosensor incubated with 800 fM (8μUmL−1) HRP was investigated. As illustrated in Table 2 , lots of amino acids, four nucleotides, and some proteins did not pose any serious interference on the determination of 800 fM (8μUmL−1) HRP. In addition, the interfering effects of other heme-containing proteins (cytochromeC, protoporphyrin IX, and myglobin) on the detection of 800 fM (8μUmL−1) HRP could also be neglected. The big π–π stacking interaction and the good spectral properties of af-GQDs-DAP complex maintain the high selectivity of this system, implying that this ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor may be applied to HRP detection in biological samples with highly selectivity.

In summary, we developed an ultrasensitive and highly selective ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor for HRP detection. The nanosensor was composed of two sections: af-GQDs as the reference fluorophore and DAP as the response signal. This ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor could realize the onsite naked eyes determination of HRP. Such ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor exhibited ultrasensitivity and highly selectivity toward HRP in a broad linear range of 2 fM–800 fM (0.02μUmL−1–8μUmL−1) with a detection limit of 0.21 fM (2.1nUmL−1) (3σ/K) under the optimum conditions, much lower than the reported values. As far as we know, this is the first time to propose such ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor for HRP based on af-GQDs. This strategy may have a broad range of desirable applications of carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials in bio-analytical areas.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21403039, 21563006), Guangxi Natural Science Foundation (2013GXNSFCA019005, 2015GXNSFAA139033), Collaborative Innovation Center of Southwest Ethnic Medicine (Guangxi Normal University, CICSEM 2013-B6), and Guangxi Colleges and Universities Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Natural Functional Molecular Chemistry, Guangxi Teachers Education University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2016.05.001.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent applications of carbon nanomaterials in fluorescence biosensing and bioimaging
A label-free photoelectrochemical aptasensor based on nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for chloramphenicol determination
A general solid-state synthesis of chemically-doped fluorescent graphene quantum dots for bioimaging and optoelectronic applications
Preparation of graphene quantum dots based core-satellite hybrid spheres and their use as the ratiometric fluorescence probe for visual determination of mercury(II) ions
Intrinsic peroxidase-like catalytic activity of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots and their application in the colorimetric detection of H2O2 and glucose
Graphene quantum dots supported by graphene nanoribbons with ultrahigh electrocatalytic performance for oxygen reduction
Optically tunable amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots
Highly luminescent flexible amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots@cellulose nanofiber–clay hybrids for white-light emitting diodes
Amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots: origin of tunable heterogeneous photoluminescence
Highly photoluminescent amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots used for sensing copper ions
A graphene quantum dot-based method for the highly sensitive and selective fluorescence turn on detection of biothiols
Graphene oxide quantum dots@silver core–shell nanocrystals as turn-on fluorescent nanoprobe for ultrasensitive detection of prostate specific antigen
Graphene quantum dots as on-off-on fluorescent probes for chromium(VI) and ascorbic acid
Visual and fluorescent detection of acetamiprid based on the inner filter effect of gold nanoparticles on ratiometric fluorescence quantum dots
Onsite naked eye determination of cysteine and homocysteine using quencher displacement-induced fluorescence recovery of the dual-emission hybrid probes with desired intensity ratio
Preparation of graphene quantum dots based core-satellite hybrid spheres and their use as the ratiometric fluorescence probe for visual determination of mercury(II) ions
Ratiometric fluorescent detection of biomakers for biological warfare agents with carbon dots chelated europium-based nanoscale coordination polymers
Dumbbell-shaped carbon quantum dots/AuNCs nanohybrid as an efficient ratiometric fluorescent probe for sensing cadmium (II) ions and l-ascorbic acid
A ratiometric nanosensor based on fluorescent carbon dots for label-free and highly selective recognition of DNA
Direct electrochemistry of horseradish peroxidase immobilized on a monolayer modified nanowire array electrode
Application of o-phenylenediamine as a fluorogenic substrate in peroxidase-mediated enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay
Spectrophotometric quantification of horseradish peroxidase with o-phenylenediamine
Femtogram detection of horseradish peroxidase by a common desktop scanner
Evaluation of seven cosubstrates in the quantification of horseradish peroxidase enzyme by square wave voltammetry
Development of an automatic multi-channel ink-jet ejection chemiluminescence system and its application to the determination of horseradish peroxidase
Evaluation of lophine derivatives as L-012 (luminol analog)-dependent chemiluminescence enhancers for measuring horseradish peroxidase and H2O2
Reversible fluorescent nanoswitch based on carbon quantum dots nanoassembly for real-time acid phosphatase activity monitoring
A real-time fluorescent assay for the detection of alkaline phosphatase activity based on carbon quantum dots
A fluorometric assay for acetylcholinesterase activity and inhibitor screening with carbon quantum dots
Fluorescence resonance energy transfer quenching at the surface of graphene quantum dots for ultrasensitive detection of TNT
Fluorescence lifetime imaging microscopy for the detection of intracellular pH with quantum dot nanosensors
Optimizing the o-phenylenediamine assay for horseradish peroxidase: effects of phosphate and pH, substrate and enzyme concentrations, and stopping reagents
Proteolytic activity monitored by fluorescence resonance energy transfer through quantum-dot-peptide conjugates
Enthalpy change and mechanism of oxidation of o-phenylenediamine by hydrogen peroxide catalyzed by horseradish peroxidase

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Shan Huang received her PhD degree in Analytical Chemistry from Wuhan University in 2009. After a two-year postdoctoral (2009–2011) stay at the Institut Armand-Frappier, Institut National de la Recherche Scientifique (INRS) and INRS-Énergie, Matériaux et Télécommunications, Université du Québec, Canada, she returned to China as an associate professor at Guangxi Teachers Education University. Her current research interests are the synthesis of functional nanomaterials and application in biochemical sensors.
Lumin Wang is pursuing her master degree in Analytical Chemistry at Guangxi Teachers Education University. She works on the development of novel nanomaterials for fluorescent sensors.

Chusheng Huang received his PhD degree in Organic Chemistry from Lanzhou University in 1998. Now, he is a professor at Guangxi Teachers Education University. His current research interests are the synthesis of natural products and their application in chemical sensors.

Wei Su received his PhD degree in Inorganic Chemistry from Shandong University in 2007. Now, he is a professor at Guangxi Teachers Education University. His current research interests are the synthesis of functional ruthenium anticancer drugs and their interactions with DNA.

Qi Xiao received his PhD degree in Physical Chemistry from Wuhan University in 2010. After a one-year postdoctoral (2010–2011) stay at University of Massachusetts, USA, he returned to China as a professor at Guangxi Teachers Education University. His current research interests are the synthesis of functional graphene nanomaterials and their application in biological sensors.
